author: @tanvirahamed5233
text:
Dear Robert,

I synthesized GO following the modified Hummers method. I did all the steps successfully. After all the purification steps, I dispersed some dried GO in DI water (2.5 g/L) and sonicated it to remove the unexfoliated graphite. I expected GO to fully disperse in water and unexfoliated graphite that would sink at the bottom after centrifugation (4000 rpm, 30 mins). I found my expected dispersion. But the problem was when I did the same steps with my GO after several days and noticed that all the GO was precipitating and could not get my expected dispersion. Is it normal? Can you walk me through these problems and let me know how I can get the perfect dispersion of my GO?

author: @sitakshigupta9624
text:
Thank you for the amazing video Robert Sir. 
I followed your GO synthesis via Tour method step-by-step and finally obtained the bright yellow mixture successfully. After that I used a centrifuge followed by washing with D.I. water, 10% HCl, ethanol (twice) and then coagulated with ether as mentioned in the Marcano paper. Then I left it to dry in a vacuum oven at 40 degree celsius. Initially I got a brown product but it wasnt completely dry so I left it to dry at the same temperature for another night. The color has now turned to brownish black and its still not dry. I suspect that there is still some acid content left but I'm stuck now. Although without actually seeing what I did it might be difficult to suggest something but if I try to crush or grind this product, will it be useful for carrying out dc conductivity studies or should I be washing it again?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you could try vacuum drying but i suspect you are right there is a residual acid content. However, graphene oxide takes a surprisingly long time to dry nd it is - to a degree - hygroscopic.

replies.author: @sitakshigupta9624
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Thank you for the prompt reply Sir. Its almost been 5 days since I left it to dry that too at about 40°C. I used to turn off the heating so that it does not get reduced due to overheating. So in case of a residual acid content if I further vacuum dry it, will the product still be safe? Or should I wash it again and then dry it further?
Sorry for being so troublesome. I just need the advice of an expert since in and around my lab, nobody has attempted GO synthesis before.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: unfortunately you are likely to need to wash it more fully - did you check pH of the wash water at the first washing stage? You will experience a degree of loss of product and reduced yield but this is just a price to pay. You are right to avoid over heating. Depending on size of starting graphite large GO will behave as a liquid crystal

replies.author: @sitakshigupta9624
replies.text:
The pH was somewhere close to 6.93. I did not measure it very accurately though. Maybe thats one of the places where I went wrong. So basically I'd have to wash the GO further and then vacuum dry it. 
Is it possible for GO to be a little dark in color? I just checked my GO has started to turn a little dark from brown, tending towards a blackish tinge but not actually black. Is it bad news? :-/ Although I didnt let it heat much for it to get reduced or damaged.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's fine

author: @jimsmindonline
text:
Sorry, more questions! Just talking about graphene, not GO.
I've been having this debate about powder forms of plain graphene.
Some say that the plain graphene doesn't restack easily so boiling off the water at the end doesn't produce much graphite.
The reading I've done suggests it takes very high temperatures to decompose graphene?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
graphene will restack - it depends on how many broken or dangeling c 
bonds are around - or at least that's what i think - if they are 
passivated by the addition of hydrogen then restacking is prevented - 
then you could just boil off the water i guess

replies.author: @jimsmindonline
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Well I tried it in true jam jar style.
Took some of my material and simply stuck it in a glass pan, brought to boil, reduced heat and simmered off the water till it was dry.
Not an ideal test but my material had a resistance of 20 ohms before and 30 ohms after.

author: @bentarr17
text:
Hi, 
Your channel is really interesting! I am hoping to make some graphene supercapacitors for a science fair project using the laserscribe method. I have a few questions about this video. I've been watching through all of your videos and I think that I want to use the tour method to make graphene.

1) Do I really need the vacuum pump? Will this process work without one?

2) Can you explain the part where you make the DIY Buchner funnel more? You said you put acrylic in it - so did you drill holes in it? Can I use hot glue instead of silicon to make a seal?

3) You said you put a spigot on the bottle. What do you mean by that? Why can't you just directly hot glue the tube to the bottle?

4) Do you think that the tour method is the easiest and safest method to make GO? If not what would you recommend? 

5) At around 17:38 you say that you dry "it" again. Do you mean the liquid in the wine bottle, or what's left on top of the filter paper?

6) You said you could dry the solution by putting on a radiator - but could you dry it without using a radiator, just by leaving it out to evaporate? How long would that take?

Thanks! Your videos have been extremely helpful.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the tour method isn't a safe method mate - i have no idea of your age or the help you have but if you haven't done much of this before seek assistance from your professor it involves handling concentrated acids and possible explosive mixtures. That being said you will never filter it without a vacuum pump - but feel free to try. If you think about what a buchner funnel is i feel you have answered your own question to number 2. By all means try it - i can only share what i did and try and save you the frustration of not making the joint properly 4 i recommend you seek help 5 what do you think? 6 yes and no idea good luck with it mate

author: @collinj2
text:
Hi Robert,
Thank you so much for all of your detailed videos, they've been a great help! 
Questions:

Is there any reason we can't use the washing methods you show here for the GO product you obtain via the Tour method? Instead of using the published DI water/HCL/Ethanol and centrifuge methods?

John

author: @joeglory425
text: Another great video!  So I was thinking about drying the GO at a low temp and that got me thinking about building a vacuum dryer (I've been thinking about that for a few years) and then I got an idea.  GO paper I hear is impermeable to gases but will let water vapor through.  So if I covered over the opening to a vacuum chamber which has water in it (and is at vacuum), heat the chamber until it is warmer than ambient temperature, will water vapor diffuse OUT of the vacuum chamber or INTO the vacuum chamber?  I welcome everyone to try this.  I'm not yet in a position to test this idea and it may be a long time before I am.  Too many pans in the fire.

replies.author: @joeglory425
replies.text: Weighing the chamber before and after at the same pressure would probably be the best way to determine which way the diffusion is going.

replies.author: @joeglory425
replies.text: I guess one would also have to account for the moisture removed by the vacuum pump.  This could be done by routing the pump discharge through a desiccant bed and doing before and after weighing of the desiccant. Keep in mind that the desiccant isn't going to remove all the moisture..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Joseph Richardson
My guess would be that the diffusion would happen across the pressure gradient much in the same way as it does in the lungs

author: @TheIdeanator
text:
How did you not mention using a dessicator bag?!

I'd put the wet GO in a bag with a load of freshly baked (and therefor dry) silica gel packets, or with relatively inexpensive dessicants from ebay. Someones selling 2.5 kilos of it for $15. In fact, epsom's salt should also work as a dessicant too...
Just a thought.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: now that's a good idea - mate - it is just not possible to think of everything and i am really not that bright lol

replies.author: @TheIdeanator
replies.text:
Glad to be of service!
I have no idea how quick that would work as I've never tried it, but it should probably speed up the process considerably. I also wonder if using a magnet in the H2SO4 dilution stage - provided you used a ferromagnetic intercallation agent - might help aid the settling process. Perhaps some magnets drifting down from the top as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
TheIdeanator
Well, it is a trick used for freeze drying if you don't have a vacuum chamber - so yeah it should work really well. I don't think the addition of a magnetic component is such a good idea though as the whole point is to get pure GO for modification for a whole load of reasons and an iron contaminant like that would ruin the GO for so many purposes.
